mappings.rationaleAI: Galaxy classification (elliptical, spiral, irregular), formation theories, collisions, supermassive black holes, the Milky Way, and galaxy clusters comprehensively address all aspects of galaxies in general (A).

The life cycle of stars including the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram, hydrogen fusion, red giants, supernovae, and nucleosynthesis addresses stellar classification (B.4), stellar statistics (D.5), structure (D.6), and evolution (D.7).

The death of stars covering planetary nebulae, supernovae, neutron stars, and stellar nucleosynthesis addresses planetary nebulae (B.5.c), supernova remnants (B.5.d), explosive variables (D.4.b.ii), and element formation (D.7.b).

The Sun's composition, nuclear fusion, spectral characteristics, photosphere/chromosphere/corona, and expected evolution provide a detailed case study addressing stellar characteristics (D.2, D.3), structure (D.6), and evolution (D.7).
Black holes at galactic centres, X-ray binaries, and stellar collapse address extragalactic X-ray sources (A.5), quasars (A.5.c), and explosive variables (D.4.b.ii).
Dark matter's role in galaxy rotation curves, gravitational lensing, and large-scale structure addresses physical properties of galaxies (A.2) and galactic structure and dynamics (B.6).
Cosmic rays as high-energy particles from astrophysical sources bombarding Earth's atmosphere directly address primary cosmic rays in the galactic composition (B.5.g).
Infrared astronomy studying cold molecular clouds, star formation, and dust around stars addresses dust clouds (B.5.e), the interstellar medium (B.5.f), star formation theories (B.7.c/D.7.a.i).

William Herschel's mapping of nebulae and the Milky Way, discovery of infrared radiation, and Caroline's comet discoveries address distance determinations (B.1), star identification (D.1), and galactic distance methods (A.1.b).

Tycho Brahe's precise stellar position cataloguing, the 1572 supernova observation, and his astronomical data address star identification (D.1), stellar positions (D.2.a), and explosive variables (D.4.b.ii).
The shape and topology of the universe, cosmic flatness, and the Omega parameter relate to the origin and evolution of galaxies within the cosmological framework (A.6).
